quick_train.headline: case_101_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on March 9, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated March 11, 2026.

After review by our medical review department, we have determined that the requested service does not satisfy Cigna's Medical Necessity Guidelines and is considered experimental and investigational. After review, we are unable to approve the requested service.

This determination is based on Cigna Medical Coverage Policy (CPG) 0234, "Pharmacologic Therapies — Coverage Criteria," version 4.2016, last reviewed June 2016 and scheduled for periodic reassessment. Under the criteria in effect, the requested medication is classified as experimental and investigational because the published evidence reviewed under this policy does not establish it as evidence-based for the submitted indication. Accordingly, the service is not eligible for coverage at this time.

You or your provider may request a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria applied to this determination at no charge by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director may be requested within the timeframe noted below.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Cigna National Appeals, P.O. Box 188011, Chattanooga, TN 37422, or by calling the number on your ID card. You may submit additional information for consideration with your appeal.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Review Department
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 61-year-old woman with chronic migraine without aura (G43.709), averaging 15 headache days per month over the past eight months, with a documented MIDAS score in the severe range. Her neurologist has trialed and optimized multiple preventive agents: topiramate titrated to 100 mg daily for three months, discontinued for disabling cognitive slowing and paresthesias; propranolol 80 mg daily for roughly ten weeks with inadequate response and symptomatic bradycardia; and three abortive triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, and eletriptan), which address acute attacks but provide no prevention. The submitted prior authorization requests erenumab (Aimovig) 70 mg subcutaneously once monthly, a CGRP-receptor antagonist FDA-approved in May 2018 for migraine prevention and supported by Category 1 pivotal randomized trials (STRIVE and ARISE) and the 2021 American Headache Society consensus statement. The specialist's letter and an eight-month headache diary documenting attack frequency, disability, and prior medication failures were included in the submission. Erenumab is indicated specifically for patients who have failed standard oral preventives, which this patient has demonstrably done.

quick_train.headline: case_145_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request for an extended suppressive course of metronidazole (twice-weekly intravaginal gel) for recurrent bacterial vaginosis and has determined that it does not meet the plan's medical necessity criteria.

An extended suppressive metronidazole course is not considered evidence-based for recurrent bacterial vaginosis under the plan's current medical policy. The published criteria the plan applies do not support authorization of suppressive therapy for this indication, and the request therefore does not satisfy the plan's coverage criteria. This determination applies even though the initial month of the suppressive course has already been dispensed and started.

Before any reconsideration, the plan requires that your physician submit additional clinical documentation, including the dates and results of the prior treated episodes. This documentation must be received within 48 hours of the date of this notice. No extensions will be granted, and if the documentation is not received within that window the determination will stand.

If you disagree with this determination, you or your provider may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 46-year-old woman with recurrent bacterial vaginosis, with four Amsel-confirmed episodes in the past twelve months despite completing a standard single course of oral metronidazole for each episode. Her gynecologist prescribed an extended suppressive regimen of twice-weekly intravaginal metronidazole gel, which CDC sexually transmitted infection treatment guidelines and randomized trials specifically endorse for recurrent bacterial vaginosis to prevent relapse, contrary to the denial's assertion that suppressive therapy is not evidence-based. The pharmacy had already dispensed, and the patient had already begun, the first month of the suppressive course at the time this retrospective denial was issued, and the denial does not state whether she is now responsible for paying for the dispensed medication. Her gynecologist submitted the dates of the four prior episodes and the record of the completed standard courses together with the request. The plan's demand that further documentation be returned within 48 hours leaves no realistic opportunity to assemble and submit those records.

quick_train.headline: case_184_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have reviewed the recent request for behavioral health services submitted on your behalf. Following review under the applicable Cigna Medical Necessity Guidelines and our Medical Coverage Policy, we have determined that the request does not meet our criteria for coverage, and it is denied as not medically necessary.

Our medical policy provides that the requested service is not considered evidence-based for the submitted indication, and the published clinical criteria do not support authorization of the requested service. In addition, our policy requires that a member first complete and document failure of the applicable course of step therapy before a service in this category is approved. The information available to us does not establish that these policy requirements have been satisfied.

This determination is based on our standard medical policy in effect on the date of review. You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria used to make this decision. Your treating provider may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director.

You have the right to appeal this decision. A written appeal may be submitted within 180 days of the date of this notice, and you may submit additional information in support of the request. Please refer to your plan documents for instructions on where to submit your appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Necessity Review
quick_train.clinical_context: The member is a 49-year-old woman with recurrent, treatment-resistant major depressive disorder dating to early 2024. She has documented inadequate response to five adequate antidepressant trials - sertraline 200 mg, venlafaxine XR 225 mg, bupropion XL 300 mg, escitalopram 20 mg, and aripiprazole augmentation - each at therapeutic dose for eight weeks or longer, alongside a course of cognitive behavioral therapy. Her two most recent PHQ-9 scores were 21 and 19. Her treating psychiatrist referred her for repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), which is FDA-cleared and recommended in the American Psychiatric Association major-depression practice guideline specifically for treatment-resistant depression after multiple failed pharmacologic trials, directly contradicting the denial's not-evidence-based characterization. The submitted records document each medication, dose, duration, and outcome, none of which the denial letter acknowledges. The fail-first step-therapy requirement the insurer imposes on this behavioral health service has no analogous requirement for comparable medical or surgical procedures under the same plan, which the treating provider has flagged as a parity concern under mental health parity rules.

quick_train.headline: case_192_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This notice concerns the request for coverage of a carfilzomib-based triplet (carfilzomib, daratumumab, and dexamethasone) administered with twice-weekly carfilzomib infusions for relapsed multiple myeloma. Cigna has completed a medical necessity review and has determined that the requested administration frequency does not meet coverage criteria.

Under the Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and the applicable MCG Care Guidelines, the requested twice-weekly carfilzomib infusion cadence exceeds the frequency we consider medically necessary for this therapy. Our reviewer concluded that a less frequent administration schedule would be expected to achieve the intended clinical benefit, and that the documentation does not establish why the more frequent cadence is required in this case. On that basis, coverage at the requested frequency is denied.

This determination reflects the visit-frequency standards described in the MCG Care Guidelines applied to your plan. The decision pertains to the cadence of administration; it is not a determination that the underlying agents are themselves excluded from coverage.

The request was received at 9:32 AM on May 19, 2026, and the determination was issued at 9:36 AM the same day, based on the information available at that time.

You may request a copy of your claim file and the clinical criteria applied to this review at no charge. You have the right to appeal this determination within 180 days of the date of this notice by submitting a written request together with any supporting clinical documentation. A peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director may be arranged on request.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 64-year-old woman with relapsed multiple myeloma (C90.00). She was initially treated with a lenalidomide-bortezomib-dexamethasone induction followed by lenalidomide maintenance, on which she progressed during the first quarter of 2026 with a serum M-spike rising from 0.4 to 1.9 g/dL over four months and a reciprocal fall in uninvolved immunoglobulins, confirming biochemical relapse. Her treating hematologist selected a carfilzomib-daratumumab-dexamethasone triplet, for which twice-weekly carfilzomib (administered on days 1, 2, 8, 9, 15, and 16 of each cycle) is the FDA-labeled and NCCN-recognized administration schedule; the dosing density is intrinsic to the regimen rather than an elective increase in visits. Renal function is preserved with a stable creatinine of 0.9 mg/dL, and an echocardiogram showed a normal ejection fraction of 60 percent, supporting tolerability of the proteasome inhibitor schedule. The oncologist documented that a reduced infusion frequency is not an established equivalent for this combination and would deliver subtherapeutic exposure for an aggressive relapse.

quick_train.headline: case_195_cigna_mednec
quick_train.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This notice concerns the request for continuous glucose monitor (Dexcom G7) supplies at the requested sensor replacement frequency submitted on your behalf. Cigna has completed a medical necessity review and has determined that the requested frequency is not approved.

The requested sensor replacement cadence exceeds the frequency supported under your plan's coverage guidelines for type 2 diabetes. In conducting this review, we applied the 2021 Cigna Medical Coverage Policy for continuous glucose monitoring, which sets the supported sensor frequency for members with type 2 diabetes. Based on that policy, the additional sensors requested above the supported cadence are not medically necessary, and the higher-frequency supply is denied.

This determination addresses only the requested frequency of sensor replacement. It does not change any previously approved coverage for the device itself at the supported cadence.

If you wish to pursue continued supply at the requested frequency, additional clinical documentation may be submitted for review under the applicable coverage policy.

You may request a copy of your claim file and the clinical criteria used in this determination at no charge. You have the right to appeal this determination within 180 days of the date of this notice by submitting a written request together with any additional records. A peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director may be arranged on request. If your appeal is unsuccessful, you may request an external review by an independent review organization.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
quick_train.clinical_context: The patient is a 49-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus with labile glucose (E11.65) on a basal-bolus insulin regimen of insulin glargine and mealtime insulin aspart. Despite this regimen he has documented nocturnal hypoglycemia, with fingerstick readings as low as 48 mg/dL confirmed on two occasions during the first quarter of 2026, alongside daytime excursions above 300 mg/dL and a most recent HbA1c of 8.7 percent, reflecting genuine glycemic lability rather than simple noncompliance. His endocrinologist prescribed a continuous glucose monitor (Dexcom G7) to capture nocturnal lows the patient cannot feel, and documented that the requested sensor replacement frequency reflects repeated early sensor failures from adhesive loss during physically active warehouse work, leaving gaps in overnight data. The record notes that current diabetes society standards and Cigna's updated coverage policy support continuous glucose monitoring at the standard sensor cadence for insulin-treated patients with hypoglycemia, and that uninterrupted monitoring is needed to safely titrate his insulin and prevent a severe hypoglycemic event.

quick_holdout.headline: case_126_cigna_mednec
quick_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your treating provider submitted for gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra Therapy) implantation evaluation to treat your diabetic gastroparesis. After review under Cigna Medical Coverage Policy CPG 0178, the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 28, 2026.

Cigna considers gastric electrical stimulation to be investigational and not evidence-based for gastroparesis. Our Medical Coverage Policy finds that the published peer-reviewed literature does not include randomized controlled trial evidence sufficient to show that the device improves net health outcomes for this indication. The coverage criteria applied here reflect efficacy data drawn predominantly from a geriatric study population (patients ages 65 and older), and the submitted records do not meet the threshold requirements in that criteria set. The requested procedure therefore does not satisfy our standard for medical necessity.

This decision was made by a Cigna medical reviewer using the information in the claim file. You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific medical necessity criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision.

If your provider would like to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a Cigna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, together with any additional clinical information you would like considered. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
quick_holdout.clinical_context: The member is a 32-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%) and a two-year history of diabetic gastroparesis. A 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy in February 2026 showed 38% retention at four hours, consistent with moderately severe delayed emptying. He has failed first-line prokinetic therapy: metoclopramide 10 mg four times daily was trialed for more than twelve weeks with persistent nausea, postprandial vomiting, and inability to maintain oral intake, and was ultimately limited by tremor and akathisia. Dietary modification to small, low-fat, low-fiber meals and a separate trial of erythromycin also failed to control symptoms. Over the preceding six months he lost approximately 7% of his body weight and required two emergency department visits for dehydration. His treating gastroenterologist documents that gastric electrical stimulation (Enterra Therapy) is indicated for refractory diabetic gastroparesis under the device's FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption, and that current peer-reviewed evidence supports its use in working-age adults whose disease is refractory to prokinetic agents. This is a young adult with treatment-refractory disease, not a patient from the geriatric cohort underlying the insurer's cited criteria.

quick_holdout.headline: case_131_cigna_mednec
quick_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the request your provider submitted for surveillance computed tomography (CT) of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis every 3 months following treatment for colon cancer. After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG), the request is denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency. Determination date: May 26, 2026.

Cigna's guidelines provide that post-treatment surveillance imaging for colorectal cancer is appropriate at an interval of every 6 to 12 months. The requested cadence of every 3 months exceeds that interval, and the submitted documentation does not establish that imaging at this higher frequency is medically necessary. The surveillance criteria applied in this review reflect colorectal cancer outcomes data in patients ages 65 and older.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific medical necessity criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a Cigna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
quick_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 38-year-old man with stage III (T3N1, 2 of 18 nodes positive) colon adenocarcinoma diagnosed at age 37, status post laparoscopic colectomy and twelve cycles of adjuvant FOLFOX completed in late 2025; tumor testing showed microsatellite-stable disease. He is now in post-treatment surveillance. His carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) has risen from 2.1 ng/mL to 4.8 ng/mL over two consecutive measurements in the first half of 2026, a trend concerning for recurrence, although cross-sectional imaging to date has been negative. His oncologist requests CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis every 3 months because young-onset colorectal cancer (diagnosis before age 50) is associated with more aggressive biology and higher early-recurrence rates, for which intensified surveillance is clinically supported. The every-6-to-12-month cadence the insurer cites is drawn from outcomes data in older patients (ages 65 and older) and does not reflect the recurrence risk of a 38-year-old with node-positive, microsatellite-stable disease and a rising tumor marker. National guidelines support more frequent imaging in higher-risk survivors, and the rising CEA further supports the requested interval.

serious_holdout.headline: case_102_aetna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
AETNA
PO Box 14079
Lexington, KY 40512

Date: June 4, 2026
Service Requested: Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) for Severe Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (F42.2)
Date(s) of Service: TBD

NOTICE OF ADVERSE BENEFIT DETERMINATION

Dear Member,

We have received your prior authorization request for an Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) for severe obsessive-compulsive disorder. We have carefully reviewed the clinical information provided by your provider.

Your request is denied. This service is not covered under your plan when provided by an out-of-network provider.

According to Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) guidelines and your specific plan documents, out-of-network behavioral health services, including IOP, are strictly excluded when in-network alternatives are available. The plan specifically excludes coverage for out-of-network treatment, and this plan exclusion supersedes any state mandates regarding parity or required coverage for OCD treatment. Because in-network alternatives for behavioral health IOP exist within the Aetna provider network, no gap exception can be granted at this time.

Therefore, we cannot approve the requested IOP with this out-of-network provider.

Reviewing Medical Director:

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.

APPEAL RIGHTS
If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to appeal. You or your authorized representative must submit your internal appeal in writing within 180 days of receiving this notice. Please include any additional medical records or documentation that you believe supports your request.

Please send your written appeal to:
Aetna Appeals Department
PO Box 14463
Lexington, KY 40512
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old male presenting with severe, treatment-resistant obsessive-compulsive disorder (F42.2). Despite adequate trials of fluvoxamine 300 mg daily for 16 weeks and clomipramine 200 mg daily for 14 weeks, along with two years of weekly outpatient Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) therapy, his Y-BOCS score remains severely elevated at 31. His treating psychiatrist determined that a specialized Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) focusing exclusively on intensive ERP for severe OCD is medically necessary to prevent further functional decline and potential inpatient hospitalization. Because there are no Aetna in-network facilities within a 50-mile radius capable of providing this specialized, high-acuity OCD programming, the provider requested authorization for an out-of-network facility and explicitly applied for a network gap exception. On May 18, 2026, the provider submitted a detailed 4-page letter of medical necessity documenting the inadequacy of the local Aetna network for this specific clinical need, as well as citing the state insurance mandate that requires fully-insured plans to provide parity and adequate network access for severe mental illness. This letter was completely ignored in the denial.

serious_holdout.headline: case_103_uhc_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
UNITED HEALTHCARE

Date of Notice: October 5, 2026

Dear Patient,

We have completed our review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider on September 1, 2026, for a Diagnostic Mammogram and Targeted Ultrasound (Diagnosis: Breast lump, BI-RADS 0, N63.0).

Request Submitted: 09/01/2026 09:00 AM EST
Review Completed: 09/01/2026 09:05 AM EST

Based on UHC medical policy, your request is denied because Medical Necessity criteria have not been met. UHC medical policy requires documented failure of prerequisite therapies before a targeted ultrasound is approved. Submitted records do not establish failure of required lower-cost options (i.e., a standard diagnostic mammogram alone). Therefore, the concurrent request for a diagnostic mammogram plus a targeted ultrasound is not covered.

You or your provider may appeal this decision within 180 days.

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 44-year-old female who presented to the clinic with a new, palpable mass in her left breast. Physical examination revealed a firm, non-mobile 1.2 cm lump in the upper outer quadrant that was not present on prior exams. Given her strong family history of breast cancer (mother diagnosed at age 49), concurrent diagnostic mammography and targeted ultrasound were ordered to comprehensively evaluate this high-risk lesion.

serious_holdout.headline: case_107_aetna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider for a non-preferred prescription medication, received at our office on April 6, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated April 8, 2026.

The requested medication is classified as non-preferred under your plan's formulary. Based on Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin (CPB) criteria and your plan's pharmacy benefit, coverage of a non-preferred agent requires that a preferred formulary alternative first be tried. Our records do not establish that a preferred lower-tier alternative has been tried, and a preferred agent remains available at a lower cost share. Accordingly, the requested medication is denied at this time.

This determination also reflects a plan exclusion: your benefit plan excludes coverage of non-preferred specialty agents where a formulary alternative has not been exhausted, and that exclusion applies to the requested service.

Our coverage criteria for this drug class reflect efficacy data characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population; on that basis the preferred formulary alternative is considered first-line, and the requested non-preferred agent is not supported for authorization at this time.

You or your provider may request a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria applied to this determination at no charge by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion with an Aetna medical director may be requested.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Aetna Appeals, P.O. Box 14463, Lexington, KY 40512, or by calling the number on your ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Pharmacy Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 47-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (G35) diagnosed in 2016, covered under a fully-insured employer-sponsored plan issued in her state of residence. Over the past two years she experienced two clinical relapses and new T2 and gadolinium-enhancing lesions on MRI while fully adherent to dimethyl fumarate 240 mg twice daily, the plan's preferred first-line oral agent, which she tolerated but to which she demonstrated inadequate disease control. Her treating neurologist escalated therapy and submitted a prior authorization for ozanimod (Zeposia), an FDA-approved oral sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator indicated for relapsing forms of MS in adults. The submission documented the prior failure of the preferred agent, baseline ECG and ophthalmologic clearance, and a current EDSS of 2.5. Ozanimod's pivotal efficacy and its FDA approval are established in adults, and adult MS disease-modifying therapy is governed by the 2018 American Academy of Neurology guideline; criteria characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population do not apply to this 47-year-old adult. Her state's step-therapy override statute requires a formulary exception when the preferred agent has already been tried and failed, a circumstance the record clearly establishes.

serious_holdout.headline: case_109_aetna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on May 18, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated May 20, 2026.

After review against our utilization management criteria, we are unable to approve the requested service. Our cardiac device coverage criteria require documentation of ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35 percent or less before an implantable defibrillator is considered medically necessary. The information available does not establish that this threshold is met, and the requested service therefore does not satisfy the applicable criteria in effect.

This determination was made under our standard medical necessity criteria for cardiac devices. No review of the member's specific diagnosis or the individual clinical records submitted with the request was performed; the determination rests solely on the coverage criterion described above.

If you disagree with this decision, you may request an appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your ID card within the time permitted under your plan.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Medical Review

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 22-year-old man with Brugada syndrome confirmed by a spontaneous type 1 Brugada pattern on resting ECG. He was referred after an episode of arrhythmic syncope without prodrome while at rest, and ambulatory monitoring captured a brief run of polymorphic ventricular ectopy. His father died suddenly at age 38, and genetic testing identified a pathogenic SCN5A variant. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed a structurally normal heart with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 62 percent, and coronary anatomy is normal for age, excluding ischemic cardiomyopathy. His electrophysiologist recommended an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator for prevention of sudden cardiac death, citing Heart Rhythm Society expert-consensus criteria, under which a spontaneous type 1 pattern with arrhythmic syncope and a family history of sudden death is a high-risk indication independent of ejection fraction. The prior authorization submission documented the ECG, the syncopal event, the family history, the SCN5A result, and the normal echocardiogram. Ejection-fraction-based criteria developed for ischemic cardiomyopathy do not apply to this channelopathy, in which sudden-death risk is arrhythmic rather than related to ventricular systolic function.

serious_holdout.headline: case_111_uhc_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

UnitedHealthcare has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider, received at our office on June 8, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated June 10, 2026.

After review against MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve the requested service. A lower level of care is appropriate for your condition: routine outpatient therapy is the indicated level of care, and the requested intensive multidisciplinary program exceeds what is required. The available documentation does not establish that outpatient management has been exhausted, and the requested service therefore does not meet the applicable criteria.

This determination was made by a UnitedHealthcare Medical Director, a family-medicine physician, based on the records available for review.

You or your provider may request a copy of the specific criteria applied to this determination by contacting the number on your member ID card. A peer-to-peer discussion may be requested.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to UnitedHealthcare Appeals, P.O. Box 740816, Atlanta, GA 30374, or by calling the number on your member ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old woman with persistent post-concussion syndrome (G44.309) five months after a motor-vehicle collision. Her daily headache, vertigo, light sensitivity, and cognitive slowing have kept her out of work and have not improved with routine outpatient care. Over the past three months she completed a course of vestibular physical therapy and saw a neuro-optometrist and a speech-language therapist, but these uncoordinated single-discipline visits did not help. Her neurologist then referred her to an intensive multidisciplinary concussion rehabilitation day program, in which neurology, vestibular therapy, neuropsychology, and occupational therapy are coordinated under one structured plan — the recommended next step once standard outpatient therapy has failed in persistent post-concussion syndrome. The submitted records document the failed outpatient trials, her Rivermead post-concussion symptom questionnaire score, and the degree of functional impairment that keeps her from returning to work.

serious_holdout.headline: case_112_cigna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on June 22, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated June 24, 2026.

After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy, we are unable to approve the requested surgical procedure. Plan policy requires that a less costly therapeutic modality be attempted before the requested procedure; a step-down approach using a lower-cost modality is considered appropriate before surgical intervention for this condition, and the records do not establish that this approach has been completed.

This determination also rests on a plan exclusion: your benefit plan excludes coverage for surgical treatment of temporomandibular joint disorders, and that exclusion applies to the requested service.

If you disagree with this decision, you may appeal. Appeals must be submitted to the Cigna Claims Service Center, P.O. Box 5555, Scranton, PA 18505, or by fax to 1-800-555-0143, within the time permitted under your plan.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Medical Review

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 48-year-old man with refractory internal derangement of the right temporomandibular joint (M26.63): chronic jaw pain, mouth opening limited to 22 mm, and joint locking, on a background of nocturnal bruxism and a remote mandibular injury. MRI shows anterior disc displacement without reduction. He is covered by a fully-insured employer plan issued in his state of residence, which by statute mandates coverage of temporomandibular joint disorders on the same basis as other joint conditions. Step-down therapy has already failed: a stabilization splint worn for six months, physical therapy, scheduled NSAIDs, and two arthrocentesis (joint lavage) procedures, none of which restored opening or relieved the locking. His oral and maxillofacial surgeon then requested TMJ arthroscopy. The practice's first appeal, mailed to the appeals address printed on the denial letter, was returned marked 'wrong department,' which delayed the appeal past the window the letter allowed.

serious_holdout.headline: case_114_aetna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has completed its medical necessity review of the prior authorization request submitted by your provider and received at our office on July 20, 2026. This letter constitutes our coverage determination dated July 22, 2026.

After review under the applicable Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin, we are unable to approve the requested service. Our medical policy requires documented failure of prerequisite therapies and lower-cost diagnostic options before the requested imaging is considered medically necessary. The submitted records do not establish that the required prerequisite options have been completed.

The appropriateness criteria applied to this determination are characterized in the ages 12 to 17 population. On that basis the requested service does not meet the applicable criteria in effect.

This determination applies the same standard criteria to all requests of this type and does not reflect any review of the member's diagnosis or the individual clinical records submitted with the request.

If you disagree with this decision, you have the right to file an internal appeal within 180 days of the date of this letter, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization. Appeals may be submitted in writing to Aetna Appeals, P.O. Box 14463, Lexington, KY 40512, or by calling the number on your ID card.

We understand this decision may be disappointing. This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Medical Review
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 32-year-old man with about a year of inflammatory back pain: morning stiffness lasting over an hour, buttock pain that alternates sides, and relief with exercise rather than rest. He is HLA-B27 positive with a raised CRP, and his rheumatologist suspects axial spondyloarthritis with sacroiliitis. Conservative management has failed; two NSAIDs at full anti-inflammatory doses, naproxen and then celecoxib, over more than three months gave no durable relief. Plain radiographs of the sacroiliac joints are normal, which is expected early in this disease because radiographic change lags years behind active inflammation. His rheumatologist ordered MRI of the sacroiliac joints without contrast to detect active sacroiliitis, the imaging step recommended for adults under the ASAS axial spondyloarthritis criteria when radiographs are non-diagnostic. Adult ASAS imaging criteria, not a pediatric or adolescent appropriateness standard, govern a 32-year-old. The submitted records document the NSAID failure, the normal radiographs, the HLA-B27 result, and the inflammatory-pain pattern.

serious_holdout.headline: case_116_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request submitted on your behalf. After applying the plan's standard utilization criteria, we are unable to approve the requested service.

The duration of the requested service exceeds the limits applicable to services of this type under your plan. Coverage of additional sessions beyond the standard course requires updated clinical documentation supporting continued treatment. The documentation required to extend coverage was not received, and on that basis the request does not meet the plan's criteria for continued coverage of the requested service.

This determination applies the same standard criteria to all requests of this type and does not reflect any review of the member's diagnosis, the ordering provider, or the individual clinical records associated with this request. The decision reflects the applicable benefit limits for the requested service.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal under the procedures described in your plan documents by contacting the number on your member ID card. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization once your internal appeal rights are exhausted.

This letter addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 62-year-old man with lumbar radiculopathy and neurogenic claudication (M54.16). MRI shows a central disc herniation contacting the traversing nerve root, correlating with his radicular leg pain and reduced walking tolerance. He has completed 18 supervised physical therapy sessions over 14 weeks, and the treating physiatrist has documented measurable gains: standing tolerance improved from 5 to 20 minutes and walking distance from one to four blocks. The physiatrist recommends continuing the structured program to consolidate progress before any surgical consideration. On May 4, 2026 the physiatrist's office submitted updated progress notes, a repeat neurologic examination, and an outcome-measure summary supporting an additional eight-week block of therapy. The patient continued attending the scheduled sessions while awaiting the plan's decision, so several visits in the requested block have already been delivered; he has asked his provider whether he will owe for those already-rendered sessions but has received no statement of any liability. There is no documentation of clinical deterioration, non-adherence, or a functional plateau that would justify stopping therapy at the standard cap.

serious_holdout.headline: case_118_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the requested temporomandibular joint procedure and is unable to approve it under your fully insured plan.

Cigna medical policy requires documented failure of prerequisite conservative therapies — including a course of occlusal splint therapy, anti-inflammatory medication, and supervised physical therapy of adequate duration — before surgical or arthroscopic management of the temporomandibular joint will be authorized. Our review of the submitted documentation did not establish that each of these required lower-cost options was completed and failed, and the request therefore does not meet the plan's criteria for authorization of this procedure. To be reconsidered, the file would need to show the specific dates, duration, and clinical response for each conservative measure attempted.

Separately, this procedure is subject to a plan exclusion for the treatment of temporomandibular joint (TMJ) disorders. On that additional basis, the procedure is not a covered benefit under your plan, independent of the medical necessity determination described above.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member ID card, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization after your internal appeal rights are exhausted.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 32-year-old man with internal derangement of the right temporomandibular joint (M26.60), with anterior disc displacement without reduction confirmed on MRI. He has persistent jaw locking, maximal incisal opening limited to 22 mm, and pain scored 7/10 that interferes with eating and speech. Over the past five months he completed a full course of conservative care: a custom occlusal splint worn nightly, a six-week trial of naproxen, and 10 sessions of jaw physical therapy, all documented as failed with no meaningful improvement in opening or pain. The oral and maxillofacial surgeon recommended arthroscopy of the joint. His coverage is a fully insured employer plan issued in Minnesota, a state that mandates coverage of medically necessary treatment for temporomandibular joint disorders on the same basis as other joints and prohibits blanket TMJ exclusions; because the plan is fully insured rather than self-funded, it is subject to that state mandate. He has already paid out of pocket for the custom splint and several of the therapy visits, and the notice does not tell him whether he will bear any further cost if he proceeds. There is no documentation that any required conservative option was skipped.

serious_holdout.headline: case_119_cigna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

This notice concerns the prior authorization request submitted by your neurologist for continued coverage of your ongoing maintenance infusion therapy. Your provider's request was received at our utilization management center on June 1, 2026 at 2:14 PM, and this determination was issued the same day at 2:17 PM.

Cigna is unable to approve the request. Continuation of this therapy requires an active prior authorization on file. The prior authorization that previously covered your treatment has lapsed, and our records do not reflect a current, approved authorization for the continued service. A new prior authorization request, accompanied by current clinical documentation, must be submitted and approved before any further doses will be covered under your plan.

This determination is based on the absence of an active authorization on file and does not by itself state a conclusion about the clinical appropriateness of your treatment.

If you disagree with this determination, you may request an internal appeal by calling the number on your member identification card. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization once internal appeal rights are exhausted. Your provider may submit a new prior authorization request at any time.

This letter addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 58-year-old man with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, diagnosed in his mid-40s and clinically and radiologically stable for several years on maintenance ocrelizumab infusions every six months, with no relapses, no new or enhancing lesions on his most recent MRI, and no features of secondary progression. His neurologist submitted current records — the treatment history, the stable MRI report, and the EDSS examination — together with the re-authorization request for the next scheduled infusion. The prior authorization lapsed for administrative reasons at the plan's renewal cycle, not because of any change in his condition or any gap in treatment need; interrupting ocrelizumab risks disease reactivation and a rebound of disability. The denial was generated three minutes after the request was received, at 2:17 PM the same afternoon, a turnaround too short for any reviewer to have read the submitted documentation. The notice does not describe the member's ERISA appeal rights, including the right to bring a civil action.

serious_holdout.headline: case_121_aetna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Aetna has completed a medical necessity review of the request submitted by your psychiatrist for a course of transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) and is unable to approve it as requested.

The duration of the requested treatment exceeds the plan's limits. Under the applicable behavioral health technology assessment policy, the authorized course is capped, and the requested number of sessions exceeds that maximum; continuation beyond the standard course requires updated clinical documentation, which was not received. On that basis the requested duration does not meet the plan's criteria for coverage.

The same policy classifies transcranial magnetic stimulation as experimental or investigational for this member, and the appropriateness criteria it applies are characterized in the adult population ages 22 to 68. The request does not satisfy those threshold requirements.

To request authorization, your psychiatrist may submit additional clinical documentation or a revised treatment plan that falls within the plan's limits.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization once internal appeal rights are exhausted.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 21-year-old man with treatment-resistant major depressive disorder, recurrent and severe, with a PHQ-9 of 23 and persistent symptoms despite adequate trials of two antidepressants (sertraline 200 mg for 14 weeks and venlafaxine XR 225 mg for 12 weeks) and an adequate course of psychotherapy. His psychiatrist recommended a standard acute course of transcranial magnetic stimulation, 36 sessions over six weeks, which is the FDA-cleared treatment protocol for major depressive disorder. TMS is FDA-cleared as an adjunct treatment for major depressive disorder in patients aged 15 and older, including young adults of his age; it is neither experimental nor restricted to patients aged 22 and older. A 36-session acute course is the standard, evidence-based course length, not an excess beyond it. The clinical records documenting the medication failures, the symptom severity, and the proposed protocol were submitted with the request. He has no seizure history or other safety concern that would preclude TMS.

serious_holdout.headline: case_122_aetna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request submitted by your provider for inpatient medically supervised alcohol withdrawal management and is unable to approve it at the requested level of care.

Plan policy requires that a less intensive, less costly modality be used when it is appropriate. Under the cited level-of-care criteria, ambulatory (outpatient) withdrawal management is the appropriate modality for most adult members, and medically managed inpatient withdrawal is characterized in the geriatric population, ages 65 and older, and in members with significant documented medical comorbidity. The submitted information does not establish that you fall within that group, and an outpatient withdrawal-management modality is therefore considered appropriate before inpatient detoxification will be authorized. Outpatient withdrawal management with scheduled clinical monitoring and a symptom-triggered medication protocol is available through the plan's network of participating providers.

To proceed, your provider may arrange outpatient withdrawal management, or may submit additional documentation supporting the requested inpatient level of care.

If you disagree with this determination, you may request an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization once your internal appeal rights are exhausted.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Behavioral Health Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 38-year-old man with severe alcohol use disorder who presented after several days of escalating withdrawal. His CIWA-Ar peaked at 17 with prominent tremor, diaphoresis, and a heart rate of 112, and he has a documented history of a prior alcohol withdrawal seizure. The evaluating emergency physician determined that outpatient (ambulatory) detoxification would be unsafe and recommended medically supervised inpatient withdrawal management. Under accepted ASAM withdrawal-management criteria, the level of care is determined by withdrawal severity, autonomic instability, and seizure risk — not by the member's age — so reserving inpatient detoxification for geriatric members misapplies the criteria to a 38-year-old who meets the clinical threshold on severity grounds. The records documenting the CIWA-Ar trend, the vital signs, the prior withdrawal seizure, and the emergency physician's safety determination were submitted with the request. The denial notice does not state the member's ERISA appeal rights, including the right to bring a civil action.

serious_holdout.headline: case_124_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request submitted by your cardiologist for renal denervation and is unable to approve it.

Coverage of this procedure requires a documented course of conservative therapy first, including optimization of antihypertensive medications and documented lifestyle modification over a defined period. The submitted documentation does not establish that the required duration and type of conservative care were completed, and on that basis the request does not meet the plan's criteria for coverage. A qualifying course must document the specific agents, the doses, the duration of each trial, and the member's measured response over the required period.

In addition, renal denervation is considered experimental or investigational under the plan's medical coverage policy for the treatment of hypertension. On that further basis, the procedure is not eligible for coverage under your plan at this time.

To request reconsideration, your cardiologist may submit documentation of the required conservative therapy or a revised treatment plan within the plan's coverage criteria.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card, and you may request an external review by an independent review organization once your internal appeal rights are exhausted.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 49-year-old woman with resistant (refractory) hypertension. Her blood pressure remains 168/104 mmHg despite maximally dosed amlodipine, chlorthalidone, and losartan — a three-drug regimen including a diuretic — taken with documented adherence, and she has completed more than a year of supervised lifestyle modification including a sodium-restricted diet, weight loss, and a structured exercise program, with ambulatory blood-pressure monitoring confirming sustained elevation. A secondary-hypertension workup was unremarkable. Her cardiologist referred her for renal denervation, which the FDA approved in November 2023 for hypertension and which current guidelines recognize as an option for resistant hypertension uncontrolled on guideline-directed therapy. The records documenting the medication regimen, the adherence, the year of lifestyle modification, and the ambulatory monitoring were submitted with the request. She has already paid out of pocket for several months of the ambulatory monitoring and the additional medication trials, and she has asked whether she would be financially responsible if she proceeds with the procedure, but the notice does not say.

serious_holdout.headline: case_134_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your provider submitted for temporomandibular joint (TMJ) arthroscopy. After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG), the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 21, 2026.

In applying the Milliman Care Guidelines, our reviewer used the criteria for orthognathic (jaw-realignment) surgery, and the submitted records do not meet the thresholds in that criteria set; on that basis the request does not satisfy our standard for medical necessity. Separately, your plan contains a plan exclusion for the treatment of temporomandibular joint disorders, which independently applies to this request.

A peer-to-peer review window was offered to your provider's office verbally by telephone; that window is not confirmed in this letter. Because the peer-to-peer was not completed, the determination stands at this stage.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal. Appeals may be sent to the address shown on your Explanation of Benefits.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 33-year-old woman with refractory internal derangement of the right temporomandibular joint, Wilkes stage III. MRI demonstrates anterior disc displacement without reduction, and she has persistent joint locking with a maximal interincisal mouth opening limited to 24 mm and pain on the right with chewing. Over the past six months she has completed conservative management - a custom stabilization splint worn nightly, twelve sessions of physical therapy with jaw exercises, and a trial of naproxen - without resolution of the mechanical locking. Her oral and maxillofacial surgeon documents that TMJ arthroscopy is indicated for internal derangement with disc displacement refractory to conservative care, and that the orthognathic-surgery criteria the insurer applied govern corrective jaw-realignment osteotomies, not arthroscopic joint procedures, so they were the wrong module for this request. Her coverage is a fully-insured plan issued in her state, where a state mandate requires coverage of temporomandibular joint disorders; that mandate preempts the plan exclusion the insurer cited. Regarding the peer-to-peer review, the surgeon's office received only a verbal telephone notice of the review window and never written confirmation, so the timeframe could not be verified or met.

serious_holdout.headline: case_135_aetna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This notice concerns a request for continued coverage that was submitted to Aetna on your behalf. Following review, Aetna is unable to approve the requested service.

Continued coverage of ongoing services requires an active prior authorization on file. Our records show that the prior authorization previously on file for this case has lapsed. Because there is no active authorization on file, continued coverage cannot be approved for the requested service.

To seek continued coverage, your provider must submit a new prior authorization with current supporting documentation. Until a new authorization is approved, the service remains not covered.

You or your provider may request a copy of the criteria used in reaching this determination. If your provider wishes to discuss this decision, a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal this decision. To file an appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter. Determination date: May 12, 2026.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 63-year-old woman with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD-7 score 16, down from 19 at intake) who has been in continuous weekly individual psychotherapy for 18 months, with documented reduction in anxiety symptoms and improved daily functioning that her psychiatrist attributes directly to the ongoing therapeutic relationship; a prior trial of escitalopram produced only partial response. The prior authorization covering this treatment expired during an active episode of care after her provider's office submitted a renewal that was not processed before the existing authorization lapsed, an administrative gap rather than a change in clinical need. Her treating psychiatrist documents that interrupting weekly psychotherapy at this stage carries a substantial risk of symptom relapse and that continuity of care is clinically necessary. The denial letter engaged with none of these facts: it did not mention her diagnosis, her treating clinician, or the submitted clinical records, and reads as generic boilerplate applicable to any case. The letter also omits the ERISA disclosures required in an adverse benefit determination, including the member's right to bring a civil action and the plan-document and claim-file rights, leaving the member without notice of those procedural protections.

serious_holdout.headline: case_149_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request submitted by your cardiologist for a cardiac resynchronization therapy defibrillator (CRT-D) and has determined that it does not meet the plan's medical necessity criteria at this time.

Under the plan's MCG criteria, cardiac resynchronization therapy is not considered evidence-based for this request, and the published criteria do not support authorization on the documentation provided. The plan's step therapy requirement directs that members complete a documented trial of sacubitril/valsartan as part of optimized guideline-directed medical therapy before device implantation is authorized. The submitted records do not establish a completed trial of sacubitril/valsartan, and on that basis the request does not satisfy the plan's criteria for coverage.

To pursue reconsideration, your cardiologist may submit documentation of the required medication trial or a revised request consistent with the plan's criteria. A revised request should address the specific criteria identified as not met and include the supporting clinical records.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. Your cardiologist may submit additional records supporting medical necessity with that request.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 64-year-old man with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. His left ventricular ejection fraction is 28 percent and his ECG shows a left bundle branch block with a QRS duration of 152 milliseconds, despite optimized guideline-directed medical therapy with carvedilol, an angiotensin receptor blocker, and spironolactone, and he remains NYHA class III. This combination of a reduced ejection fraction with a wide left bundle branch block is a class I guideline indication for cardiac resynchronization therapy. The step therapy the denial demands is a trial of sacubitril/valsartan; however, sacubitril/valsartan is contraindicated for this patient because he has a documented history of angioedema with angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor and ARNI exposure, which is why he is maintained on an angiotensin receptor blocker instead. Requiring a contraindicated medication before authorizing the indicated device is not consistent with his documented history. His cardiologist submitted the echocardiogram, the ECG, and the medication history with the request.

serious_holdout.headline: case_150_aetna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request for the requested service. After review, the request is denied and the requested service is not approved.

The annual visit limit for outpatient therapy services under this plan has been reached for the current plan year. Additional visits beyond the annual maximum are not a covered benefit this plan year. In addition, the requested service is classified under the habilitative services benefit category, which is excluded from coverage under the terms of this plan.

Coverage of outpatient therapy services is subject to the annual visit maximum and the benefit categories defined in your plan documents, regardless of the provider or setting in which the services are delivered.

This determination applies the plan's standard criteria to all requests of this type and does not reflect any review of the member's diagnosis or individual clinical records. No additional case-specific findings were made.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting the number on your member identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional information with that request.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 34-year-old man who underwent arthroscopic partial medial meniscectomy of the right knee eight weeks ago and was referred for a continued course of post-operative physical therapy to restore quadriceps strength, range of motion, and gait mechanics that were lost after the injury and surgery. Because this therapy restores function the patient previously had, it is rehabilitative care, not habilitative care, which develops new functional skills a patient never acquired; the denial's classification of the therapy under the habilitative services benefit category is therefore incorrect. His orthopedic surgeon documented persistent quadriceps weakness, a 15-degree extension lag, and an antalgic gait, and recommended additional supervised physical therapy beyond the plan's annual visit cap to complete the recovery. The denial letter contains no reference to his diagnosis, his surgeon, the operative report, or any of the submitted progress notes, and states that it does not reflect any review of the individual records. The operative report and the physical therapy progress notes were submitted with the prior authorization request.

serious_holdout.headline: case_152_cigna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Cigna has reviewed the prior authorization request for continued outpatient neurological rehabilitation visits, including physical and occupational therapy for gait and balance, submitted on your behalf. After review, the request is denied.

The annual visit limit for outpatient rehabilitation therapy services under this plan has been reached for the current plan year. Additional visits beyond the annual maximum are not a covered benefit for the remainder of the plan year.

The visit-frequency criteria applied in this determination are characterized in the geriatric (ages 65 and older) population, and under those criteria the rehabilitation visits already furnished this plan year are considered sufficient. The request for additional supervised therapy therefore exceeds the frequency supported by the applicable criteria.

Coverage of outpatient rehabilitation services is subject to the annual visit maximum and the medical-management frequency criteria described in your plan documents, regardless of the provider or setting in which the services are delivered.

If you disagree with this determination, you may request an appeal. Written appeals must be submitted to the Cigna Pharmacy Appeals Unit, P.O. Box 42005, Phoenix, AZ 85080, or by fax to 1-800-555-0143, within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional clinical information with your appeal.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 52-year-old man with young-onset Parkinson disease and motor fluctuations, including roughly six hours of daily off-time and freezing of gait despite optimized oral levodopa. His neurologist requested continued multidisciplinary physical and occupational therapy beyond the plan's annual visit cap to address gait freezing, postural instability, and two recent falls, which a prior supervised course measurably improved. The visit-frequency criteria the denial applies are drawn from a geriatric population characterized as ages 65 and older; the American Academy of Neurology and Parkinson Foundation rehabilitation recommendations support intensive, individualized therapy for working-age patients with motor fluctuations and do not endorse a geriatric-frailty visit cap for a 52-year-old who remains employed and ambulatory. The appeals contact printed on the denial is the Cigna Pharmacy Appeals Unit; under the member's plan documents, medical-necessity appeals must be directed to the Medical Appeals unit, and the pharmacy unit returns misdirected medical appeals.

serious_holdout.headline: case_157_cigna_mednec
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Ms. Member,

Cigna has completed a medical necessity review of the request for belimumab (Benlysta) intravenous infusion. After review, the request is denied.

Based on our coverage policy, a lower level of care is appropriate for the management of systemic lupus erythematosus at this time, and the requested intravenous biologic infusion exceeds what is required. Under the criteria in our 2019 lupus management coverage policy, standard outpatient pharmacologic management is expected to be optimized and exhausted before an intravenous biologic agent is authorized, and the documentation reviewed does not establish that this lower level of care has been exhausted.

Continued management with standard outpatient therapy is therefore the appropriate level of care at this time. If your condition does not respond to optimized outpatient management, a future request may be submitted with additional documentation for review.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional clinical information with your appeal, and a clinician who was not involved in this determination will review your appeal.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Cigna Utilization Management
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The patient is a 22-year-old woman with active systemic lupus erythematosus, with a SLEDAI score of 10, inflammatory arthritis, and a malar rash, whose disease remains active despite hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily and mycophenolate mofetil titrated to 2 grams daily over the past eight months. Her rheumatologist requested belimumab (Benlysta) as add-on therapy for persistently active disease. Standard outpatient management has in fact been optimized and exhausted: she remains clinically active on maximized hydroxychloroquine and mycophenolate, which is precisely the basis for escalation. The denial relies on the plan's 2019 lupus coverage policy; the current 2023 EULAR recommendations for the management of systemic lupus erythematosus endorse belimumab as add-on therapy for patients with active disease inadequately controlled by standard immunosuppressants, superseding the older policy. The denial letter also omits any notice of the member's right to independent external review.

serious_holdout.headline: case_188_cigna_priorauth
serious_holdout.denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request your provider submitted on May 22, 2026 for renal denervation (catheter-based renal sympathetic denervation) to treat resistant (refractory) hypertension (I10). After review, this request is not approved at this time.

The requested procedure is to be performed by an out-of-network provider. Under the terms of your plan, out-of-network services are not covered without an approved network gap exception, and our records do not reflect that such an exception has been granted. In-network options for managing your blood pressure are available, and on that basis the out-of-network request is not authorized.

Under our current Cigna Medical Coverage Policy, renal denervation is moreover considered experimental or investigational for this indication. We also applied our geriatric hypertension management criteria, under which the requested procedural intervention is not supported. For these reasons the request does not meet our criteria for approval.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria relied upon. Your treating provider may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director. You have the right to appeal this decision in writing within 180 days of the date of this notice. Please refer to your plan materials for instructions on where to submit your appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Prior Authorization Review
serious_holdout.clinical_context: The member is a 48-year-old woman with true resistant hypertension, with office blood pressures persisting around 168/104 mmHg and a mean 24-hour ambulatory pressure of 152/96 mmHg, despite maximally tolerated doses of three first-line agents from different classes - amlodipine 10 mg, chlorthalidone 25 mg, and losartan 100 mg - plus documented adherence on pharmacy refill data. A spironolactone trial was limited by hyperkalemia, and secondary causes were excluded. Her in-network cardiologist referred her for renal denervation because no in-network facility in her plan's network performs the procedure, and submitted a network gap-exception request with the prior authorization. Renal denervation received FDA approval in November 2023 for the treatment of hypertension and is supported in current adult AHA/ACC hypertension guidance for resistant hypertension, directly contradicting the experimental or investigational characterization. The geriatric hypertension management criteria the plan applied, which emphasize relaxed blood-pressure targets and deprescribing for frail elderly patients, do not apply to a 48-year-old adult, for whom guideline-based targets and treatment escalation are appropriate.
